title: Race conditions and synchronization

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Race conditions and synchronization; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; JVM/runtime concepts, Concurrency, and Virtual Threads learning paths; atomic topic: Race conditions and synchronization
sources.claim: A Java data race occurs when threads access shared mutable state without the required ordering; compound actions need one synchronization or atomicity strategy that preserves the invariant.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java data race occurs when threads access shared mutable state without the required ordering; compound actions need one synchronization or atomicity strategy that preserves the invariant.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Two threads increment a shared int and lose updates, so the design confines counts per task and combines results after join. Your goal is to expose and repair a deterministic shared-state race in a teaching example. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Packages, builds and tests; List, Set, Map and Queue; Threads, tasks and executors; References, heap and garbage collection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java data race occurs when threads access shared mutable state without the required ordering; compound actions need one synchronization or atomicity strategy that preserves the invariant. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: volatile gives visibility and ordering but not atomicity for read-modify-write, synchronized locks by monitor identity, concurrent collections protect documented operations, and callbacks under locks can deadlock. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Declaring a counter volatile makes counter++ atomic and therefore protects every invariant that includes the counter.

includes: Recognize non-atomic compound actions and prefer confinement or immutable messages over shared mutation.
Expose and repair a deterministic shared-state race in a teaching example.
excludes: volatile gives visibility and ordering but not atomicity for read-modify-write, synchronized locks by monitor identity, concurrent collections protect documented operations, and callbacks under locks can deadlock.

outcomes: Expose and repair a deterministic shared-state race in a teaching example.

examples.title: Race conditions and synchronization: direct language case
examples.situation: Two threads increment a shared int and lose updates, so the design confines counts per task and combines results after join.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Two threads increment a shared int and lose updates, so the design confines counts per task and combines results after join.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: volatile does not make compound actions atomic; confine mutable state, use an appropriate atomic operation, or synchronize the entire invariant transition.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because volatile does not make compound actions atomic; confine mutable state, use an appropriate atomic operation, or synchronize the entire invariant transition.

examples.title: Race conditions and synchronization: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: An AtomicInteger makes one counter increment atomic, while a multi-field account transfer still uses a lock around the complete invariant.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: An AtomicInteger makes one counter increment atomic, while a multi-field account transfer still uses a lock around the complete invariant.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: volatile gives visibility and ordering but not atomicity for read-modify-write, synchronized locks by monitor identity, concurrent collections protect documented operations, and callbacks under locks can deadlock.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Declaring a counter volatile makes counter++ atomic and therefore protects every invariant that includes the counter.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Expose and repair a deterministic shared-state race in a teaching example.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Declaring a counter volatile makes counter++ atomic and therefore protects every invariant that includes the counter.
misconceptions.correction: volatile does not make compound actions atomic; confine mutable state, use an appropriate atomic operation, or synchronize the entire invariant transition.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Declaring a counter volatile makes counter++ atomic and therefore protects every invariant that includes the counter.

analogy.example: a dispatch center that tracks reachable work orders and assigns bounded tasks while protecting shared ledgers can introduce Race conditions and synchronization, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reachability, executors, races, and virtual-thread workload choice
limitations: memory visibility, scheduling, atomicity, cancellation, and garbage-collection timing cannot be inferred from human dispatch

recap.summary: You should now be able to expose and repair a deterministic shared-state race in a teaching example. Keep this boundary in view: volatile gives visibility and ordering but not atomicity for read-modify-write, synchronized locks by monitor identity, concurrent collections protect documented operations, and callbacks under locks can deadlock.
